papers.title: Exploring the Potential of Residual Impurities in Germanium Detectors for MeV-Scale Dark Matter Detection
papers.abstract: The direct detection of MeV-scale dark matter (DM) particles hinges on achieving an exceptionally low-energy detection threshold. Germanium (Ge) detectors, meticulously tailored with precise impurity compositions, hold the potential to enhance sensitivity to energy levels below the sub-electronvolt (sub-eV) range. This study explores the behavior of residual impurities inherent to Ge detectors at helium temperatures, unveiling a captivating freeze-out phenomenon leading to the formation of excited localized states known as dipole states. Using compelling evidence from relative capacitance measurements obtained from two detectors, we elucidate the transition of impurity atoms from free charge states to these dipole states as the temperature drops from 11 to 6.5 K. Our investigation comprehensively covers the intricate formation of these dipole states in both n-type and p-type impurities. Furthermore, we shed light on the electric field generated by these dipole states, revealing their ability to trap charges and facilitate the creation of cluster dipole states. Confirming findings from previous measurements, we establish that these excited dipole states exhibit a binding energy of less than 10 meV, offering an exceptionally low detection threshold for MeV-scale DM. Building upon this concept, we propose the development of a 1-kg Ge detector with internal charge amplification—an innovative approach poised to surpass electrical noise and enable the detection of MeV-scale DM with unprecedented sensitivity.

papers.title: Pushing the Limits: Single Electron-Hole Pair Detection with an Ultra-Low Threshold Germanium Detector
papers.venue: 2023 IEEE Nuclear Science Symposium, Medical Imaging Conference and International Symposium on Room-Temperature Semiconductor Detectors (NSS MIC RTSD)
papers.abstract: This paper presents a novel germanium detector with an extremely low energy threshold and the capability of detecting a single electron-hole pair, making it suitable for exploring new physics such as low-mass dark matter searches and new properties of neutrinos. The detector utilizes phonon excitation of dipole and cluster dipole states when operated at liquid helium temperature at which residual impurities in the germanium detector undergo freeze-out from the conduction or valence band, creating localized states that form thermally stable dipole and cluster dipole states. These states have binding energies below 10 meV for both p-type and n-type germanium detectors. This low binding energy corresponds to about 2 THz of phonon frequency, making it suitable for infrared radiation detection. The detector generates single electron-hole pairs when the trapped charge carriers are released through deformation of dipole or cluster dipole states. These carriers are drifted under an electric field toward a point contact, where the highest electric field strength is established. Depending on the bias voltage, the amplification gain factor can be controlled from a factor of 100 to 1000 or higher, enabling the detector to overcome electronic noise. This detector can be sensitive to low-mass dark matter down to 1 keV/c2 and low-energy neutrinos such as solar neutrinos and reactor neutrinos for coherent elastic neutrino-nucleus scattering. Additionally, the detector can provide finger fingerings for materials as an instrumentation tracker.

papers.title: Investigating binding energies and trapping cross-sections in an n-type Ge detector at low temperatures
papers.venue: Frontiers in Detector Science and Technology
papers.abstract: We investigated charge transport in an n-type germanium detector at 5.2 K to explore new technology for enhancing low-mass dark matter detection sensitivity. Calculations of dipole and cluster dipole state binding energies and electric field-dependent trapping cross-sections are critical to developing low-threshold detectors. The detector operates in two methods: depleting at 77K before cooling, or directly cooling to 5.2 K and applying different bias voltages. Results indicated lower binding energy of charge states in the second method, at zero field and under an electric field, suggesting different charge states formed under different operating methods. Measured cluster dipole and dipole state binding energies at zero field were 7.88± 0.64 meV and 8.37± 0.75 meV, respectively, signifying high low-threshold potential for low-mass dark matter searches in the future.
